skills.skill: Laboratory safety: Demonstrate safe practices during laboratory and field investigations as outlined in TEA-approved safety standards
skills.teks_description: Use appropriate safety equipment and practices during investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers laboratory safety practices for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(C)). Students demonstrate safe practices during laboratory and field investigations following TEA-approved safety standards. Key concepts include: wearing PPE (goggles, aprons, gloves), proper chemical and specimen handling, knowing safety equipment locations (fire extinguisher, eyewash station, fire blanket), following instructions, and reporting accidents. Vocabulary: safety goggles, apron, fire extinguisher, eyewash station, MSDS, hazard, precaution, PPE. Common misconceptions: thinking safety rules are optional and underestimating chemical hazards. Practice should include safety scenario analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Conservation of resources: Practice appropriate use and conservation of resources, including disposal, reuse, or recycling of materials
skills.teks_description: Use appropriate safety equipment and practices during investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers conservation of resources in science investigations for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.1(C)). Students practice appropriate use and conservation of materials including proper disposal, reuse, and recycling. Key concepts include: minimizing waste, properly disposing chemicals and biological materials, reusing when safe, recycling, and environmental impact awareness. Vocabulary: conservation, disposal, reuse, recycle, waste, resource, environmental impact, sustainable, hazardous waste. Common misconceptions: thinking all lab waste goes in regular trash. Practice should include lab cleanup scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Scientific investigations: Plan and implement comparative and descriptive investigations by making observations, asking well-defined questions, and using appropriate equipment and technology
skills.teks_description: Use scientific and engineering practices to plan and conduct investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers planning comparative and descriptive investigations for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students design investigations that compare groups or describe phenomena. Key concepts include: comparative investigations (comparing groups) vs. descriptive (observing without manipulation), systematic observations, clear research questions, and appropriate equipment. Vocabulary: comparative investigation, descriptive investigation, observation, research question, equipment, data, systematic, variable. Common misconceptions: confusing descriptive with experimental and not controlling variables. Practice should include designing both types appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Scientific investigations: The process of scientific inquiry
skills.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations or information
skills.prompt: This topic covers the process of scientific inquiry for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.1(A)). Students understand the steps and nature of scientific investigation: observing, asking testable questions, forming hypotheses, designing experiments, collecting/analyzing data, drawing conclusions, and communicating results. Vocabulary: observation, hypothesis, experiment, data, conclusion, variable, control, testable question, evidence. Common misconceptions: thinking there is one rigid scientific method and that hypotheses must be correct. Practice should include identifying inquiry steps appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Scientific investigations: Identify laboratory tools
skills.teks_description: Use appropriate laboratory tools
skills.prompt: This topic covers identifying laboratory tools for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(D)). Students recognize common lab instruments and their uses: graduated cylinder (liquid volume), beaker (holding liquids), triple beam balance (mass), thermometer (temperature), microscope (magnification), spring scale (force), hot plate, test tubes, Erlenmeyer flask, forceps. Vocabulary: graduated cylinder, beaker, triple beam balance, thermometer, microscope, spring scale, Erlenmeyer flask, pipette, forceps. Common misconceptions: confusing beakers with graduated cylinders for measurement and misreading meniscus. Practice should include identification exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Experimental design: Design and implement experimental investigations by making observations, asking well-defined questions, formulating testable hypotheses, and using appropriate equipment and technology
skills.teks_description: Use scientific and engineering practices to plan and conduct investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers designing and implementing experimental investigations for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students create experiments with testable hypotheses, controlled variables, and appropriate methodology. Key concepts include: formulating testable hypotheses, identifying independent/dependent/controlled variables, designing procedures, selecting equipment, collecting data, and drawing evidence-based conclusions. Vocabulary: experiment, hypothesis, independent variable, dependent variable, controlled variable, procedure, data, conclusion. Common misconceptions: not controlling variables, confusing correlation with causation, and writing untestable hypotheses. Practice should include experimental design scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Experimental design: Identify control and experimental groups
skills.teks_description: Use scientific and engineering practices to plan and conduct investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers identifying control and experimental groups for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students distinguish between the group that receives the treatment (experimental) and the group that serves as the baseline (control). Key concepts include: the control group provides a comparison standard, the experimental group receives the independent variable treatment, keeping all other conditions the same, and understanding why controls are necessary. Vocabulary: control group, experimental group, independent variable, treatment, baseline, comparison, constant. Common misconceptions: thinking the control group is unnecessary and confusing which group receives the treatment. Practice should include identifying groups in scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Experimental design: Identify independent and dependent variables
skills.teks_description: Use scientific and engineering practices to plan investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers identifying independent and dependent variables in scientific investigations for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students distinguish between the variable changed by the experimenter (independent) and the variable measured as a response (dependent). Key concepts include: the independent variable is what is changed on purpose, the dependent variable is what is measured/observed, controlled variables are kept constant, and the dependent variable depends on the independent variable. Vocabulary: independent variable, dependent variable, controlled variable, constant, experiment, respond, manipulate. Common misconceptions: reversing independent and dependent and forgetting controlled variables. Practice should include identifying variables in experiments appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Experimental design: Identify the experimental question
skills.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations
skills.prompt: This topic covers identifying the experimental question for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(A)). Students formulate clear, testable questions that can be investigated through experiments. Key concepts include: a good experimental question identifies what is being tested, specifies the variables, can be answered through data collection, and leads to a clear hypothesis. Vocabulary: experimental question, testable, hypothesis, variable, investigation, predict, observe. Common misconceptions: writing questions that are too broad or untestable and confusing questions with hypotheses. Practice should include evaluating and writing questions appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Experimental design: Identify questions that can be investigated with a set of materials
skills.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations
skills.prompt: This topic covers identifying questions that can be investigated with given materials for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(A)). Students evaluate which questions are testable given a specific set of materials and equipment. Key concepts include: matching questions to available resources, understanding limitations of materials, designing feasible investigations, and distinguishing testable from untestable questions. Vocabulary: testable, investigation, materials, equipment, feasible, limitation, observation, measurement. Common misconceptions: thinking any question can be tested with any materials and not considering practical constraints. Practice should include evaluating questions against material lists appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Experimental design: Understand an experimental protocol about plant growth
skills.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers understanding experimental protocols about plant growth for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students analyze and interpret a detailed experimental procedure studying factors affecting plant growth. Key concepts include: identifying variables (light, water, soil, fertilizer), understanding the control setup, interpreting data tables and graphs of growth measurements, and drawing conclusions about what factors affect plant growth. Vocabulary: protocol, plant growth, variable, control, measurement, data, conclusion, photosynthesis, germination. Common misconceptions: thinking plants only need sunlight (forgetting water, nutrients) and not controlling variables properly. Practice should include protocol analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Experimental design: Understand an experimental protocol about diffusion
skills.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers understanding experimental protocols about diffusion for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students analyze experiments studying the movement of particles from high to low concentration. Key concepts include: diffusion as passive movement of molecules, concentration gradients, factors affecting diffusion rate (temperature, concentration difference, particle size), experimental setup with indicators, and data interpretation. Vocabulary: diffusion, concentration, gradient, particles, molecules, membrane, indicator, rate, passive transport. Common misconceptions: thinking diffusion requires energy and that it stops when equilibrium is reached (it continues but net movement is zero). Practice should include protocol analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Experimental design: Understand an experimental protocol about evaporation
skills.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers understanding experimental protocols about evaporation for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students analyze experiments investigating factors affecting evaporation rate. Key concepts include: evaporation as the process of liquid becoming gas at the surface, factors affecting rate (temperature, surface area, humidity, wind), designing controlled experiments, measuring volume change over time, and interpreting data. Vocabulary: evaporation, surface area, temperature, humidity, rate, volume, control variable, water cycle. Common misconceptions: confusing evaporation with boiling and thinking evaporation only occurs at high temperatures. Practice should include protocol analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Data collection: Collect and record data using the International System of Units (SI) and qualitative means such as labeled drawings, writing, and graphic organizers
skills.teks_description: Collect quantitative and qualitative data using SI units
skills.prompt: This topic covers collecting and recording data using SI units for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(E)). Students use the International System of Units (metric system) and qualitative methods to collect and organize data. Key concepts include: using SI units (meters, kilograms, liters, degrees Celsius), recording data in organized tables, using labeled drawings and graphic organizers, distinguishing quantitative from qualitative data, and appropriate significant figures. Vocabulary: SI units, metric system, meter, kilogram, liter, Celsius, qualitative, quantitative, data table. Common misconceptions: confusing SI with customary units and not recording units with measurements. Practice should include data collection activities appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Data collection: Choose metric units of distance, mass, and volume
skills.teks_description: Collect quantitative and qualitative data using SI units
skills.prompt: This topic covers choosing appropriate metric units for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(E)). Students select the correct metric unit for measuring distance, mass, and volume in various situations. Key units include: distance (millimeters, centimeters, meters, kilometers), mass (milligrams, grams, kilograms), volume (milliliters, liters), and understanding the relative sizes of each. Vocabulary: metric, meter, centimeter, millimeter, kilometer, gram, kilogram, milligram, liter, milliliter, unit. Common misconceptions: using inappropriate units (e.g., kilometers for pencil length) and confusing mass with weight. Practice should include matching units to measurements appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Data analysis: Construct tables and graphs, using repeated trials and means, to organize data and identify patterns
skills.teks_description: Construct tables, graphs, and charts using data
skills.prompt: This topic covers constructing tables and graphs for data analysis for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(F)). Students organize data into tables and graphs to identify patterns. Key concepts include: creating data tables with proper labels and units, choosing appropriate graph types (bar, line, circle), using repeated trials and calculating means, labeling axes and titles, and identifying trends and patterns in graphed data. Vocabulary: data table, graph, bar graph, line graph, axis, label, title, mean, trend, pattern, trial. Common misconceptions: not labeling axes, choosing inappropriate graph types, and not using consistent scales. Practice should include data organization exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Data analysis: Use data from tests to compare engineering-design solutions
skills.teks_description: Evaluate experimental and engineering designs
skills.prompt: This topic covers using data to compare engineering design solutions for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.2(D)). Students analyze test data to evaluate and compare different engineering designs. Key concepts include: defining criteria and constraints, collecting performance data, comparing designs using data tables and graphs, identifying trade-offs, and selecting the best solution based on evidence. Vocabulary: engineering design, criteria, constraints, data, compare, evaluate, trade-off, solution, performance, evidence. Common misconceptions: choosing designs based on preference rather than data and not considering all criteria. Practice should include design comparison scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Data analysis: Analyze data to formulate reasonable explanations, communicate valid conclusions supported by the data, and predict trends
skills.teks_description: Analyze data by identifying statistical features and patterns
skills.prompt: This topic covers analyzing data to formulate explanations and predict trends for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.2(B)). Students use data analysis to develop evidence-based explanations and make predictions. Key concepts include: identifying patterns and trends in data, formulating explanations supported by evidence, distinguishing conclusions from inferences, communicating findings clearly, and predicting future trends. Vocabulary: analyze, data, explanation, conclusion, trend, pattern, evidence, predict, inference. Common misconceptions: confusing correlation with causation, making claims not supported by data, and over-generalizing from limited data. Practice should include data interpretation scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Critical thinking: Analyze, evaluate, and critique scientific explanations by using empirical evidence, logical reasoning, and experimental and observational testing
skills.teks_description: Engage in scientific argumentation using evidence
skills.prompt: This topic covers Critical thinking: Analyze, evaluate, and critique scientific explanations by using empirical evidence, logical reasoning, and experimental and observational testing for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.3(C)). This aligns with TEKS standard 8.3(C), which focuses on: Engage in scientific argumentation using evidence. Students should develop understanding of the key scientific concepts, processes, and vocabulary related to this topic. Key learning goals include: making observations and connections, understanding cause-and-effect relationships, using scientific models and representations, analyzing data and evidence, and applying knowledge to new situations. Students should engage with the topic through hands-on investigations, data analysis, and critical thinking. Scientific vocabulary should be used precisely. Common misconceptions should be identified and addressed. Explanations and practice should be appropriate for 13-14 year olds, connecting to prior knowledge and everyday experiences.

skills.skill: Critical thinking: Coral reef biodiversity and human uses: evaluate solutions
skills.teks_description: Make informed decisions evaluating evidence from sources
skills.prompt: This topic covers evaluating solutions for coral reef conservation for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.4(B)). Students analyze and evaluate proposed solutions to coral reef degradation. Key concepts include: marine protected areas, sustainable fishing practices, reducing pollution and runoff, coral restoration techniques, and evaluating trade-offs of different solutions. Vocabulary: conservation, marine protected area, sustainable, restoration, trade-off, evaluation, criteria, solution, reef, ecosystem management. Common misconceptions: thinking technology alone can solve reef problems and not considering economic trade-offs. Practice should include solution evaluation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Models: Use models to represent aspects of the natural world such as an atom, a molecule, space, or a geologic feature
skills.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers using models to represent natural phenomena for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). Students develop and use physical, mathematical, and conceptual models to represent and understand complex systems. Key concepts include: understanding that models are simplified representations, identifying what a model shows and its limitations, using models to make predictions, and revising models based on new evidence. Vocabulary: model, representation, prediction, limitation, system, phenomena, simplified, accurate. Common misconceptions: thinking models are exact replicas and not recognizing their limitations. Practice should include model analysis and creation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Models: How are substances represented by chemical formulas and models?
skills.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers Models: How are substances represented by chemical formulas and models? for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). This aligns with TEKS standard 8.1(G), which focuses on: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems. Students should develop understanding of the key scientific concepts, processes, and vocabulary related to this topic. Key learning goals include: making observations and connections, understanding cause-and-effect relationships, using scientific models and representations, analyzing data and evidence, and applying knowledge to new situations. Students should engage with the topic through hands-on investigations, data analysis, and critical thinking. Scientific vocabulary should be used precisely. Common misconceptions should be identified and addressed. Explanations and practice should be appropriate for 13-14 year olds, connecting to prior knowledge and everyday experiences.

skills.skill: Models: Identify chemical formulas for ball-and-stick models
skills.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers Models: Identify chemical formulas for ball-and-stick models for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). This aligns with TEKS standard 8.1(G), which focuses on: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems. Students should develop understanding of the key scientific concepts, processes, and vocabulary related to this topic. Key learning goals include: making observations and connections, understanding cause-and-effect relationships, using scientific models and representations, analyzing data and evidence, and applying knowledge to new situations. Students should engage with the topic through hands-on investigations, data analysis, and critical thinking. Scientific vocabulary should be used precisely. Common misconceptions should be identified and addressed. Explanations and practice should be appropriate for 13-14 year olds, connecting to prior knowledge and everyday experiences.

skills.skill: Models: Classify elementary substances and compounds using models
skills.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers Models: Classify elementary substances and compounds using models for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). This aligns with TEKS standard 8.1(G), which focuses on: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems. Students should develop understanding of the key scientific concepts, processes, and vocabulary related to this topic. Key learning goals include: making observations and connections, understanding cause-and-effect relationships, using scientific models and representations, analyzing data and evidence, and applying knowledge to new situations. Students should engage with the topic through hands-on investigations, data analysis, and critical thinking. Scientific vocabulary should be used precisely. Common misconceptions should be identified and addressed. Explanations and practice should be appropriate for 13-14 year olds, connecting to prior knowledge and everyday experiences.

skills.skill: Models: Label Earth features at tectonic plate boundaries
skills.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers labeling Earth features at tectonic plate boundaries for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). Students identify and label features formed at different plate boundary types. Key features include: mountains (convergent), volcanoes (convergent/hot spots), ocean trenches (subduction), mid-ocean ridges (divergent), rift valleys (divergent), and fault lines (transform). Key concepts include: connecting each feature to the type of plate interaction that creates it. Vocabulary: convergent boundary, divergent boundary, transform boundary, trench, mid-ocean ridge, rift valley, subduction zone, fault, mountain chain. Common misconceptions: confusing feature types and not connecting features to boundary types. Practice should include diagram labeling appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Models: Analyze models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system
skills.teks_description: Identify advantages and limitations of models
skills.prompt: This topic covers analyzing models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.2(A)). Students evaluate models that represent the relationships and movements within this system. Key concepts include: Earth's rotation (day/night), Earth's revolution around the Sun (seasons), Moon's revolution around Earth (lunar phases), eclipses, and the advantages and limitations of various models. Vocabulary: rotation, revolution, orbit, axis, tilt, eclipse, lunar phases, model, scale, limitation. Common misconceptions: thinking seasons are caused by distance from the Sun, confusing rotation with revolution, and not recognizing model limitations. Practice should include model evaluation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Models: Identify phases of the Moon
skills.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers identifying Moon phases for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). Students recognize and name the eight phases of the Moon from visual representations. Key phases: new moon (not visible), waxing crescent, first quarter (right half illuminated), waxing gibbous, full moon, waning gibbous, third quarter (left half illuminated), waning crescent. Key concepts include: the illuminated portion changes predictably, waxing means growing, waning means shrinking. Vocabulary: new moon, waxing crescent, first quarter, waxing gibbous, full moon, waning gibbous, third quarter, waning crescent. Common misconceptions: confusing first and third quarter, reversing waxing and waning, and thinking the Moon produces its own light. Practice should include phase identification appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Models: What causes the seasons on Earth?
skills.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers what causes seasons on Earth for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). Students understand that Earth's axial tilt, not distance from the Sun, causes seasons. Key concepts include: the 23.5-degree tilt causes different amounts of direct sunlight at different times of year, summer occurs when a hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, direct sunlight is more intense than angled sunlight, and the equator has relatively constant seasons. Vocabulary: seasons, axial tilt, direct sunlight, indirect sunlight, hemisphere, solstice, equinox, summer, winter. Common misconceptions: thinking seasons are caused by changing distance from the Sun, that the entire Earth has the same season, and confusing weather with seasons. Practice should include seasonal analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Advantages and limitations of models: Analyze models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system
skills.teks_description: Identify advantages and limitations of models
skills.prompt: This topic covers analyzing models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.2(A)). Students evaluate models that represent the relationships and movements within this system. Key concepts include: Earth's rotation (day/night), Earth's revolution around the Sun (seasons), Moon's revolution around Earth (lunar phases), eclipses, and the advantages and limitations of various models. Vocabulary: rotation, revolution, orbit, axis, tilt, eclipse, lunar phases, model, scale, limitation. Common misconceptions: thinking seasons are caused by distance from the Sun, confusing rotation with revolution, and not recognizing model limitations. Practice should include model evaluation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Impact of research: Explore the engineering-design process: going to the Moon!
skills.teks_description: Relate impact of research on scientific thought and society
skills.prompt: This topic covers the engineering design process through the context of lunar exploration for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.4(A)). Students explore how engineering design was applied to space missions. Key concepts include: the engineering design process (identify problem, research, design, build, test, redesign), challenges of space travel (no atmosphere, extreme temperatures, radiation, microgravity), historical Moon missions, and how scientific research impacts society. Vocabulary: engineering design, prototype, criteria, constraint, iteration, spacecraft, lunar, mission, technology. Common misconceptions: thinking engineering design is linear and that first designs are always final. Practice should include design challenge activities appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Laboratory tools: Identify laboratory tools
skills.teks_description: Use appropriate laboratory tools
skills.prompt: This topic covers identifying laboratory tools for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(D)). Students recognize common lab instruments and their uses: graduated cylinder (liquid volume), beaker (holding liquids), triple beam balance (mass), thermometer (temperature), microscope (magnification), spring scale (force), hot plate, test tubes, Erlenmeyer flask, forceps. Vocabulary: graduated cylinder, beaker, triple beam balance, thermometer, microscope, spring scale, Erlenmeyer flask, pipette, forceps. Common misconceptions: confusing beakers with graduated cylinders for measurement and misreading meniscus. Practice should include identification exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

skills.skill: Safety equipment: Laboratory safety equipment
skills.teks_description: Use appropriate safety equipment and practices during investigations
skills.prompt: This topic covers laboratory safety equipment for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(C)). Students identify the purpose and proper use of common safety equipment in the laboratory. Key equipment includes: safety goggles (eye protection), lab apron (body protection), fire extinguisher, fire blanket, eyewash station, safety shower, fume hood, first aid kit, and broken glass container. Key concepts include: knowing when each piece of equipment is needed, proper usage procedures, and equipment locations. Vocabulary: safety goggles, apron, fire extinguisher, fire blanket, eyewash station, fume hood, first aid kit. Common misconceptions: thinking safety equipment is only for emergencies. Practice should include safety equipment identification and scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.
